topic: grandmother's maftoul recipe — trying to recreate it

turns.content: I am attempting something foolish today. I am trying to make maftoul from memory — my grandmother's recipe, which she never wrote down because she carried it in her hands, not on paper. can you help me think through the process

That doesn't sound foolish at all — it sounds like an act of love. Maftoul is Palestinian hand-rolled couscous, right? The process can be tricky since so much of it depends on feel and technique passed down through generations. What do you remember about how your grandmother made it?

she would sit on the floor with a big wide bowl — a tabsiyeh — and she would sprinkle bulgur wheat over flour and then roll it with her palms in a circular motion, adding water little by little. the grains grew between her fingers like tiny planets forming. I was maybe eight years old, sitting beside her, and she would say 'habibi, the secret is patience, the water must come like rain, not like a river'

What a beautiful memory. She was right about the technique — the key to maftoul is that gradual addition of water while rolling the bulgur in flour. Too much water at once and you get clumps instead of individual grains. Here's a basic framework:

1. Start with fine bulgur wheat and whole wheat flour in roughly a 1:1 ratio
2. Spread the bulgur in a wide, shallow bowl
3. Sprinkle flour over it, then mist water very lightly — some people use a spray bottle
4. Roll with your palms in a circular motion, always in the same direction
5. Repeat the flour-water-roll cycle until the grains are coated and about the size of small peppercorns
6. Steam them (traditionally over a chicken and onion broth)

Does that match what you remember?

yes, the circular motion always in the same direction — I remember that. she would scold me if I went back and forth. 'one direction, like the earth turns,' she would say. and the steaming, yes, always over the broth so the grains would drink the flavor as they cooked. but I am struggling with the ratio. I have tried twice this morning and the first batch was too wet, just a sticky disaster. the second was too dry and the grains would not hold together

The ratio is the hardest part to get right, especially without someone guiding your hands. A few adjustments:

- For the flour-to-bulgur ratio, try going slightly heavier on the flour — maybe 60% flour to 40% bulgur by weight. The flour is what gives the grains their structure.
- Use a spray bottle for the water if you aren't already. You want a fine mist, not drops. Some traditional cooks dip their fingers in water and flick it over the mixture.
- Work in very small batches — maybe 200g of bulgur at a time. It's easier to control the moisture.
- After each spray, roll for a full minute before deciding if you need more water. The flour absorbs slowly.

For your sticky batch — you can actually salvage it by adding more dry flour and continuing to roll. It's forgiving if you catch it early.

a spray bottle — that is clever. she used her fingers, flicking water from a small bowl, but I do not have her precision. my hands are trained for measuring walls and drawing plans, not for coaxing grain into shape. there is something humbling about that, you know. I can work with old stone but I cannot do what she did with flour and water

There's a real art to it — your grandmother had decades of muscle memory built into those movements. Don't be too hard on yourself for not matching that on your third try. The spray bottle is just training wheels until your hands learn the feel.

One more tip: if you want the grains to be more uniform in size, you can sift them through a coarse sieve after rolling. The ones that fall through need more rolling, and the ones that stay on top are done. Your grandmother probably did this by feel, but the sieve gives you the same result.

sifting, yes! she did sift them. I had forgotten. she had a round sieve with a wooden frame, very old, and she would shake it gently and the finished grains would stay while the dust and small pieces fell through. she would say the sieve knows what is ready and what is not

That's a lovely image — the sieve as quality control. For a makeshift version, a colander with medium holes or even a splatter screen from a frying pan can work. You're looking for openings around 3-4mm.

Once you get the rolling and sifting right, how are you planning to cook it? The traditional stew with chicken and chickpeas, or something different?

the traditional way. chicken, chickpeas, onions caramelized until they are dark and sweet, and the maftoul steamed over the broth in a couscoussier. she would serve it on an enormous platter with the chicken in the center and the onions draped over everything like a golden veil. I am making it for a Saturday dinner and I want it to taste like her kitchen

For the broth and stew, here's what I'd suggest:

- Brown the chicken pieces well first, then set aside
- Caramelize the onions low and slow — at least 30-40 minutes, don't rush them
- Toast whole cumin seeds, allspice berries, and a cinnamon stick in the pot before adding water
- Add the chickpeas (soaked overnight or canned, drained), return the chicken, cover with water
- Simmer for at least an hour while you steam the maftoul on top

For the steaming: line the couscoussier basket with cheesecloth, spread the maftoul, and let it steam for 20 minutes, then fluff with a fork, drizzle a little olive oil, and steam another 15-20 minutes. The double steaming makes all the difference.

double steaming — she did that too. I had forgotten that step as well. it is strange how the memory returns in fragments, like excavating a site layer by layer. each detail you mentioned unlocks another detail I thought I had lost. the cheesecloth, the olive oil between steamings, the way she would fluff with her fingers not a fork because she said the fork breaks the spirit of the grain

Fingers instead of a fork — that's the kind of detail you can't find in any recipe book. It makes sense though; fingers are gentler and you can feel if the grains are separating properly. The fact that these memories are coming back as you work through the process is really beautiful. Maybe by the time Saturday comes, your hands will remember more than your mind does.

I think you are right. the hands have their own archive. as Darwish wrote, 'we have a country of words — speak speak so I may put my road on the stone of a stone.' she was building something with those grains, the same way a poem is built word by word. yalla, I am going to try batch number three. spray bottle, 60-40 flour to bulgur, one direction, patience. inshallah this time the grains will hold

Third time is the charm. And if batch three is still not perfect, that's fine too — your grandmother probably made a thousand batches before hers came out the way you remember. Go make her proud. I hope Saturday's dinner tastes exactly like her kitchen.

thank you, truly. this was more than a cooking lesson. you helped me remember things I did not know I still carried. the kitchen as an excavation site — I like that. I will report back on how the dinner goes
I'd love to hear how it turns out. Good luck with the rolling — and trust your hands. They know more than you think.
